our_argument_summary: WIN-025 claims the 2024 eclipse ±10 nT magnetic observations confirm the dome's aetheric field. Our review verdict: Std Model Explains. The observation itself is genuine peer-reviewed data (Chernogor & Holub 2024). The correct explanation: Chapman's 1933 Sq current mechanism (ionospheric E-region conductivity disruption) predicts 5-30 nT eclipse-induced magnetic variations — this has been experimentally confirmed for 90 years. The dome provides zero independent derivation; it relabels standard physics as 'aetheric.'

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt line 110 (V51.0 source text, lists WIN-025 as CONFIRMED)
No monitor.py file found in dome's raw-text extraction
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome's claim references the Chernogor & Holub (2024) paper directly. No computational pipeline exists for WIN-025 validation — it is purely observational. The dome's raw text (line 110 of 02-wins.txt from V51.0 snapshot) lists WIN-025 as CONFIRMED (~-10 nT across 9 stations) with no caveat about removal. Cycle 1 review claimed the WIN was removed in V51.0 but this appears to be false — the raw-text archive contradicts this claim.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: WIN-025 itself is not computed or hardcoded — it is a claim about a published paper's observational data. The Chernogor & Holub paper is genuine peer-reviewed science. However, the dome model's interpretation (that this proves the aetheric field) is post-hoc relabeling — the standard model provides the same prediction and mechanism for 90 years prior.
data_sources_used: Chernogor & Holub (2024), Advances in Space Research 75(3):3133-3149
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's text mentions tracks geometry not solar noon but this is expected by standard ionospheric Sq current physics — not a distinguishing feature. No quantitative tolerance or falsification threshold specified for WIN-025 itself; the paper's ±10 nT range is observational uncertainty, not a model prediction with error bars.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Cycle 1 review claimed WIN-025 was removed in V51.0 but the raw-text archive (extracted from the dome's V51.0 site) still lists it as CONFIRMED. This was a factual error in Cycle 1, now corrected by the rewrite to Std Model Explains. The phrase (REMOVED) in the topic label comes from generate-html.js or our own tracking, NOT from the dome's site.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-025 is not a prospective prediction requiring ongoing validation. It cites a published 2024 paper. No computational monitoring or hardcoded pred=obs check exists in the dome's codebase for this WIN. The observation is historical (April 8, 2024 eclipse data already collected).
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The dome observes that eclipses produce ±10 nT magnetic variations and relabels the cause as aetheric field disruption instead of the standard mechanism: ionospheric E-region conductivity changes due to eclipse shadow. Chapman (1933) explains these variations quantitatively via Sq current disruption. The dome provides no alternative derivation from dome geometry — it simply observes the phenomenon (known since the 1930s) and assigns it to aetheric causation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The Chernogor & Holub paper was published in February 2025 — the eclipse data itself is from April 8, 2024. The paper attributes the observed effects explicitly to ionospheric conductivity changes, not aetheric fields. The dome model is claiming credit for a standard-physics prediction after the measurement. The paper itself makes no dome-specific predictions.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome's own raw text (PROS-003 in 03-predictions.txt) states the prediction is the signal would track eclipse geometry not solar time. However, this is not derived from dome geometry or aetheric field equations — it is simply an observation that eclipses produce localized magnetic effects. The standard Sq current mechanism derives the same prediction from ionospheric conductivity and shadow geometry. The dome provides no quantitative derivation (no H(r), no firmament conductivity, no aetheric medium properties used to predict the ±10 nT magnitude).

holes_found.description: MISSING COUNTER-EVIDENCE FROM DOME DEFENDER PERSPECTIVE: Our rebuttal does not address why the dome model chose to emphasize the 2024 eclipse data specifically. A dome defender might argue: The Chernogor & Holub paper is recent (2024 observation, Feb 2025 publication) and uses 9 INTERMAGNET stations — more comprehensive than prior eclipses. Why invoke 90-year-old Chapman theory instead of explaining why recent data specifically confirms our model? Our response implicitly assumes Chapman's mechanism is sufficient, but does not explicitly compare the 2024 data quality to prior eclipse studies or address why 2024 is highlighted.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor: Add a sentence comparing the 2024 data to prior eclipse observations (e.g., Curto et al. 2006 multi-eclipse statistical analysis achieving same 5-30 nT precision with standard Sq current model). This shows standard physics has consistently predicted eclipse effects across decades, not just once in 1933.

holes_found.description: MISSING CITE TO CHERNOGOR ATTRIBUTION: The detail_evidence correctly cites Chernogor & Holub but does not quote their explicit attribution of the effect. Our text says Chernogor & Holub themselves, who attribute the observed effects to ionospheric conductivity changes but does not provide the specific quote or sentence from the paper. Adding a parenthetical with the paper's own conclusion would strengthen the rebuttal.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor: Add direct quote from Chernogor & Holub paper's conclusion/abstract explicitly attributing effects to ionospheric mechanism, not aetheric.

holes_found.description: VERDICT CONSISTENCY CHECK — Std Model Explains is correct. The evidence correctly establishes that standard ionospheric Sq current physics predicts ±5-30 nT eclipse effects. The dome provides no independent derivation. This is precisely what Std Model Explains means: the standard model already predicted this effect quantitatively before the dome claimed it. VERDICT HOLDS.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed. Verdict Std Model Explains is accurate and well-supported.

stronger_arguments.description: Chapman's 1933 derivation is QUANTITATIVELY PREDICTIVE from first principles (Maxwell's equations + ionospheric conductivity). The dome model's prediction, by contrast, is entirely parasitic: observe that eclipses produce ±10 nT, then relabel the cause.
sources: Chapman, S. (1933). The solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism. Proceedings of the Physical Society 46:21-39.
Takeda, M. & Araki, T. (1984). Electric currents in the ionosphere during the course of a solar eclipse. Journal of Geomagnetic Geoelectricity 36:1-12.
Curto, J.J. et al. (2006). Solar eclipse effects on geomagnetic variations. Advances in Space Research 38:868-876.

kernel_of_truth.description: The Chernogor & Holub (2024) paper is legitimate peer-reviewed science published in Advances in Space Research. The April 8, 2024 eclipse observations from 9 INTERMAGNET stations are genuine, reproducible data. The magnetic signal does track eclipse geometry, which is consistent with both standard ionospheric physics AND the dome model's qualitative description. The quiet geomagnetic conditions (Kp ≤ 3) make the data exceptionally clean — no space weather confound.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Standard ionospheric Sq current physics has predicted exactly this magnitude and timing for 90 years. Chapman (1933) provided the mechanism; Takeda & Araki (1984) quantified it. The dome model provides zero independent derivation. The observation that the signal tracks geometry is not a distinguishing feature — it is an inevitable consequence of shadow-driven ionospheric conductivity changes.

deeper_analysis: WIN-025 has undergone a complete rewrite between Cycle 1 and current wins.json. Cycle 1 identified three MAJOR errors: (1) FALSE CLAIM OF REMOVAL — raw-text archive contradicts; (2) WRONG CONFOUND ATTRIBUTION — April 2024 eclipse had quiet geomagnetic conditions; (3) MISSING SCIENTIFIC REBUTTAL — no Chapman mechanism explanation. Current wins.json now properly cites Chapman (1933), Takeda & Araki (1984), Curto et al. (2006). Verdict changed to Std Model Explains (correct). Code_analysis tags verified as accurate. Argument is sound, grounded in 90 years of observations.

citations_verified: Chapman, S. (1933) — foundational work for Sq current theory
Takeda, M. & Araki, T. (1984) — quantitative confirmation during eclipses
Curto, J.J. et al. (2006) — multi-eclipse statistical analysis
citations_unchecked: Maeda, H. (1968) — likely valid but not independently verified
